questions.body: What is the prevalence of primary adrenal insufficiency?
questions.ideal_answer: The prevalence of primary adrenal insufficiency is estimated at between 82 and 144 cases per million people. Auto-immunity is the most common cause in adults, while genetic causes, particularly enzyme defects, are the most common cause in children. By comparison, secondary adrenal insufficiency has a higher estimated prevalence of 150-280 per million. Acute adrenal crisis occurs in both primary and secondary adrenal insufficiency, with an incidence of approximately 6-8 per 100 patients per year in Europe, and a prospective study reported 0.5 deaths per 100 patient-years from adrenal crisis. Long-term morbidity is not well established, but alterations in quality of life in both physical and mental health components have been demonstrated in multiple studies.

questions.body: What is adductor canal syndrome?
What are the main types of DNA damage induced by UV radiation?
questions.ideal_answer: UV radiation induces several main types of DNA damage. The most well-characterized are pyrimidine photoproducts, specifically cyclobutane pyrimidine dimers (CPDs), including the thymine-thymine cyclobutane dimer (T-T), and the thymine-thymine pyrimidine-pyrimidone (6-4) photoproduct (T (6-4) T), also called the (6-4) photoproduct. These arise from intrastrand and interstrand DNA cross-links between adjacent pyrimidine bases. UV exposure also induces DNA strand breaks, which are early intermediates in the repair process and can severely impair cellular activities if not fully rejoined. Additionally, sister chromatid exchange and general DNA breakage have been documented in UV-exposed cells, such as preimplantation embryos. Together, these lesions, if left unrepaired, can block DNA replication, cause mutations, and contribute to conditions such as skin cancer and xeroderma pigmentosum.

questions.body: What are indications of quetiapine?
questions.ideal_answer: Quetiapine is an atypical antipsychotic with a broad range of indications. Its approved indications include schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, generalized anxiety disorder, severe depression, dementia, and mood disorders. It is also indicated for multiple drug abuse (MDA), which in one retrospective study was the most common indication (46.2%), followed by psychosis (29.7%) and depression (24.1%). Off-label uses documented in psychiatric hospital settings include organic mental disorders, disorders due to psychoactive substance use, and affective disorders. Quetiapine was among the most frequently prescribed off-label drugs for organic mental disorders and disorders due to psychoactive substance use in a large German multicenter study.
exact_answer: disorders due to psychoactive substance use

questions.body: In GBM, which cell state is associated with the loss of Neurofibromin (NF1)?
questions.ideal_answer: Loss of Neurofibromin (NF1) in GBM is strongly associated with the mesenchymal cell state. Multiple lines of evidence support this: NF1 alterations are usually associated with the mesenchymal molecular subtype of GBM, and NF1 loss in patient-derived glioma cells promotes mesenchymal subtype and epithelial-mesenchymal transition-specific gene expression. Mechanistically, NF1 loss activates the RAS/MAPK pathway, which upregulates the AP-1 transcription factor FOSL1, a key regulator of the mesenchymal subtype. Single-nucleus RNA sequencing of NF1-mutant GBMs further demonstrates that mesenchymal-like (MES-like) tumor cells are enriched for MEK activation signatures, and MEK inhibition selectively depletes these MES-like cells. Additionally, tumors with NF1 genetic loss express the mesenchymal marker podoplanin. Depletion of FOSL1 in NF1-mutant cells results in loss of the mesenchymal gene signature, confirming the causal link between NF1 loss and mesenchymal transformation in GBM.

questions.body: What the recurrence percentage for Cushing's Disease?
questions.ideal_answer: The recurrence rate for Cushing's disease following transsphenoidal surgery varies considerably across studies, ranging from 3% to 46%, depending on the definition criteria used and the duration of follow-up. Commonly cited ranges are 5% to 20% and 15% to 25%. One recent study from a pituitary tumor center of excellence reported a recurrence rate of 27.4% after a median follow-up of 38 months. Epidemiological data indicate that late recurrences occur in up to 20% of patients, with the risk not plateauing even after 20 years of follow-up. Half of recurrences tend to appear within 5 years of surgery, with the remainder occurring within or even after 10 years. Protective factors against recurrence include lower immediate post-operative cortisol concentrations, longer duration of glucocorticoid replacement, and the presence of microadenomas rather than macroadenomas. Given the unpredictable timing of recurrence, lifelong follow-up is mandatory for all patients with Cushing's disease.

questions.body: What causes neuronal ceroid lipofuscinoses (NCLs)?
questions.ideal_answer: Neuronal ceroid lipofuscinoses (NCLs) are rare, inherited lysosomal storage disorders caused by aberrant lysosomal function, leading to progressive neurodegeneration and the accumulation of autofluorescent ceroid lipopigment. They are genetically heterogeneous, encompassing various types from CLN1 to CLN14 (excluding CLN9), each linked to a particular mutated gene. For example, CLN7 and CLN8 are caused by pathogenic mutations confirmed by whole exome sequencing, while CLN11 results from homozygous pathogenic mutations in the progranulin gene (GRN). CLN2 disease involves deficiency of an enzyme treatable by enzyme replacement therapy. Mitochondrial impairment, including dysfunctional oxidative phosphorylation and altered mitochondrial membrane potential, has also been implicated in NCL pathogenesis. The disorders are inherited, often arising in the context of consanguinity, and are characterized by progressive neurological deterioration including epilepsy, cognitive decline, vision loss, and motor dysfunction.

questions.body: Does the PI4KIIalpha of Drosophila melanogaster show homology with that of Homo sapiens?
questions.ideal_answer: Yes, the PI4KIIalpha of Drosophila melanogaster shows homology with that of Homo sapiens. Abstract 3 explicitly describes this novel class of phosphatidylinositol 4-kinases as highly conserved evolutionarily from yeast to humans, indicating that the Drosophila and human PI4KII enzymes are homologous. Abstract 1 further supports this by characterizing both rat PI4KIIalpha and Drosophila PI4KII as members of the same enzyme family, demonstrating that the PI4KII family extends beyond mammalian sources. Although PI4KIIs bear essentially no sequence similarity to other protein or lipid kinases and represent a novel and distinct branch of the kinase superfamily, they are conserved within their own family across diverse organisms including insects and mammals. The functional characterization of Drosophila PI4KII, including its catalytic domain and its role in phosphatidylinositol 4-phosphate production, further confirms the evolutionary conservation of this enzyme family between Drosophila melanogaster and Homo sapiens.

questions.body: Vitamin deficiencies in severe alcoholism.
questions.ideal_answer: Severe alcoholism is strongly associated with several vitamin deficiencies, primarily due to malnutrition, poor dietary intake, and altered metabolism. Thiamine (vitamin B1) deficiency is the most prominent, leading to conditions such as Wernicke's encephalopathy, Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome, and beriberi (both dry and wet forms, including beriberi heart disease). Alcohol abuse is the leading risk factor for thiamine deficiency, which can cause serious neurological and cardiovascular complications. Additionally, deficiencies in folate, vitamin B-6, and vitamin B-12 are prevalent in individuals with alcoholic liver disease, as these vitamins are essential for hepatic methionine metabolism. Chronic ethanol exposure disrupts this metabolic pathway, contributing to the pathogenesis of alcoholic liver disease through alterations in S-adenosylmethionine regulation and epigenetic changes in genes relevant to liver injury. Together, these vitamin deficiencies underscore the importance of nutritional assessment and supplementation in the management of severe alcoholism.

questions.body: Can tumor cells be reprogrammed into immune cells?
questions.ideal_answer: Yes, tumor cells can be reprogrammed into immune cells. Research has demonstrated that tumor cells can be converted in vivo into type 1 conventional dendritic cells by delivering the transcription factors PU.1, IRF8, and BATF3 via adenoviral vectors. These reprogrammed tumor cells gain the ability to present antigens, remodel the tumor microenvironment, recruit and expand polyclonal cytotoxic T cells, induce tumor regressions, and establish long-term systemic immunity in multiple mouse melanoma models. Importantly, in human tumor spheroids and xenografts, this reprogramming proceeded independently of immunosuppression, which typically limits conventional immunotherapy approaches. These findings suggest that in vivo reprogramming of tumor cells into immunogenic dendritic-like cells represents a promising strategy for cancer immunotherapy and has paved the way for potential human clinical trials.

questions.body: What are the typically biomedical characteristics of primary adrenal insufficiency?
questions.ideal_answer: Primary adrenal insufficiency is characterized by a deficiency of all adrenocortical hormones, which distinguishes it from secondary and glucocorticoid-induced forms. Diagnostically, it typically presents with low early-morning serum cortisol levels, specifically below 5 µg/dL, reflecting impaired cortisol production by the adrenal cortex. High corticotropin (ACTH) levels are concurrently observed, as the pituitary gland attempts to stimulate the failing adrenal glands. Additionally, low dehydroepiandrosterone sulfate (DHEAS) levels are characteristic, reflecting impaired adrenal androgen production. These findings contrast with secondary and glucocorticoid-induced adrenal insufficiency, which typically show low or intermediate cortisol levels with low or low-normal corticotropin and DHEAS levels.

questions.body: Please list the common clinical characteristic of the Heidenhain variant of Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease (HvCJD)
questions.ideal_answer: The hallmark clinical characteristic of the Heidenhain variant of Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (HvCJD) is the presence of prominent visual symptoms at disease onset, typically preceding cognitive impairment and other neurological deficits by weeks. These visual disturbances most commonly manifest as blurred vision, progressive loss of visual acuity, and a decline in visual fields, including homonymic hemianopsia. Patients may also experience spells of visual dysfunction and, particularly in genetic HvCJD, cortical blindness. As the disease progresses, additional features emerge including cognitive decline, memory impairment, ataxia, paranoia, behavioral changes, and visual hallucinations. Seizures can occur but are uncommon, reported in only about 5.5% of cases. The disease follows a rapidly progressive and fatal course, with death often occurring within weeks to months of diagnosis. The initial presentation of unexplained visual symptoms without corresponding MRI findings should raise clinical suspicion for HvCJD.
exact_answer: prominent visual features early in clinical course

questions.body: Does whole-chromosome silencing occur in somatic cells?
questions.ideal_answer: Yes, whole-chromosome silencing occurs in somatic cells through a process known as X chromosome inactivation (XCI). In female mammals, one of the two X chromosomes is transcriptionally silenced in somatic cells to achieve dosage compensation. The long non-coding RNA XIST plays a central role, coating the inactive X chromosome and recruiting silencing complexes. Evidence from multiple studies confirms XCI in diverse somatic cell types: adult human B cells require continuous XIST activity to silence X-linked immune genes such as TLR7; somatic cell hybrids demonstrate that mouse genes like Zfx, Rps4, and Ube1x are expressed only from the active X; mosaic mice with Piga mutations show XCI across heart, lung, kidney, brain, and liver; and cloned bovine calves display X-linked gene silencing patterns in somatic organs. Additionally, X inactivation can spread into autosomal material in X;autosome translocations, silencing autosomal genes in somatic cell hybrids. These findings collectively demonstrate that whole-chromosome silencing is an ongoing, actively maintained process in somatic cells, not merely a developmental event confined to early embryogenesis.

questions.body: Are eating disorders common in male adolescents?
What is the estimated incidence of endogenous Cushing's Syndrome in the human population?
questions.ideal_answer: The estimated incidence of endogenous Cushing's Syndrome ranges from 2 to 8 per million people annually. A second source estimates the incidence more narrowly at 2 to 4 cases per million inhabitants per year. Cushing's disease, the most frequent cause of endogenous Cushing's Syndrome, accounting for approximately 60-70% of cases, has an incidence of between 1.2 and 2.4 cases per million inhabitants per year and is 3 to 8 times more common in women than in men. ACTH-independent Cushing's Syndrome accounts for about 20% of endogenous cases, most frequently due to adrenal adenomas or carcinomas. The condition is associated with hyperglycemia, hypertension, central obesity, protein catabolism, immunosuppression, and characteristic phenotypical changes such as facial plethora, easy bruising, and purple striae.
exact_answer: 2-4 cases per million inhabitants per year
1.2 to 2.4 cases per million inhabitants per year

questions.body: Please list the types of medication errors
questions.ideal_answer: Medication errors encompass a wide range of types identified across various healthcare settings. These include delivery to the incorrect patient and privacy breaches (common in pharmacy home delivery), dosing errors and wrong or improper drugs (frequently seen in pediatric emergency departments), wrong infusion rate, unauthorized medication, and wrong time errors (prevalent among ICU nurses). Documentation errors and dose errors are common among nursing students during medication administration. Additional error types include wrong dose, wrong technique, wrong route, self-medication errors, and wrong administration of medication by family members. Near-misses are also reported. Specific drug classes frequently involved include anti-infectives, vaccines, analgesics, blood substitutes and perfusion solutions, and psycholeptics, particularly in pediatric populations. Contributing factors across settings include knowledge deficits, deviation from procedures, lack of patient information, interruptions, insufficient training, and workflow gaps.
exact_answer: wrong administration of the medication by family members

questions.body: What is the most common genotoxic factor that humans are exposed to?
questions.ideal_answer: The most common genotoxic factor humans are exposed to is solar ultraviolet (UV) radiation. Solar radiation is Earth's primary natural source of UV radiation and is consistently identified across the provided abstracts as a major environmental genotoxic factor. UV radiation, particularly UVB (280-320 nm) and UVA (320-400 nm), induces genotoxicity through multiple pathways: direct DNA damage resulting in cyclobutane pyrimidine dimers (CPDs) and pyrimidine (6-4) pyrimidone photoproducts, generation of reactive oxygen species such as singlet oxygen leading to oxidative lesions like 8-oxoguanine, immunosuppression, and inflammation. UVB is primarily responsible for direct DNA damage including pyrimidine dimer formation, while UVA acts largely through indirect photosensitization mechanisms. Solar UV radiation is linked to photocarcinogenesis, photoaging, and amphibian decline, underscoring its broad biological impact. Visible light also contributes to photocarcinogenesis via oxidative pathways, but UV radiation remains the predominant genotoxic component of solar radiation to which humans are most commonly and significantly exposed.

questions.body: What are the recommended screening tests to diagnose the presence of Cushing's Syndrome?
questions.ideal_answer: The recommended screening tests to diagnose Cushing's Syndrome, as endorsed by the Endocrine Society's Clinical Practice Guidelines, are the 1 mg dexamethasone suppression test (DST), late-night salivary cortisol (LNSC), and 24-hour urinary free cortisol (UFC). The 1 mg DST evaluates the normal feedback reaction of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis; a morning serum cortisol level below 50 nmol/L can exclude Cushing's syndrome unless clinical suspicion is high. Late-night salivary cortisol measures cortisol at its normal nadir, which is disrupted in Cushing's syndrome, resulting in abnormally elevated nighttime values. Urinary free cortisol reflects integrated tissue exposure to free cortisol over 24 hours and is particularly useful in severe hypercortisolism. Guidelines recommend using a single test with high diagnostic accuracy as an initial screen, with the choice individualized based on patient characteristics and lifestyle. Each test has procedural limitations, including drug interference with DST, potential false results with salivary cortisol, and the need for complete urine collection for UFC.

questions.body: Is Seborrhea a condition that affects the gastrointestinal tract?
questions.ideal_answer: Seborrhea and seborrheic dermatitis are conditions that affect the skin, not the gastrointestinal tract. Seborrheic dermatitis is a common, chronic inflammatory skin condition that targets sebaceous gland-rich areas such as the scalp, face, and body folds. Its multifactorial etiology involves sebocyte activity, skin microbiome dysbiosis, and immune factors. Seborrhea, or oily skin, refers to excessive sebum production on the skin surface. Treatments for these conditions include topical antifungals, corticosteroids, calcineurin inhibitors, and agents like chitosan nanoparticles that reduce skin sebum levels. None of the available evidence suggests any involvement of the gastrointestinal tract in seborrhea or seborrheic dermatitis; these are purely dermatological conditions.

questions.body: What impact could biosynthetic meat have on human health?
What are common blood cell changes caused by elevated cortisol levels?
questions.ideal_answer: Elevated cortisol levels, as seen in Cushing's syndrome, commonly cause several changes in blood cell counts. These include leukocytosis (increased total white blood cell count) and neutrophilia (increased neutrophil counts), along with an elevated neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR). Conversely, lymphocyte counts tend to decrease, resulting in relative lymphopenia, and eosinophil counts are also reduced. Some studies additionally report increases in the platelet-to-lymphocyte ratio (PLR) and monocyte-to-lymphocyte ratio (MLR). Total leukocyte count correlates positively with cortisol levels, while lymphocyte percentage shows a negative correlation, reflecting immune suppression under hypercortisolemic conditions. These alterations are observed across different Cushing's syndrome subtypes and age groups, and many partially normalize following surgical remission, though WBC and neutrophil counts may remain elevated compared to healthy controls even after treatment.

questions.body: is Rheumatic heart disease caused by Staphylococcus Aureus?
questions.ideal_answer: Rheumatic heart disease (RHD) is not caused by Staphylococcus aureus. RHD is a complication of acute rheumatic fever (ARF), which is triggered by an abnormal autoimmune response to Group A Streptococcus (GAS) infection. Following a GAS infection, molecular mimicry causes the immune system to attack host tissues, particularly the heart, leading to inflammation and valvular damage. RHD is the leading cause of valvular heart disease globally and can result in serious complications such as mitral regurgitation, heart failure, and atrial fibrillation. Prevention relies on early antibiotic treatment of GAS infections and long-term prophylactic antibiotics to prevent recurrent ARF episodes. Vaccine development efforts, including mRNA-based approaches targeting M proteins of GAS, are underway to reduce the global burden of GAS infections and their post-infectious complications, including ARF and RHD. Staphylococcus aureus plays no role in the pathogenesis of RHD.

questions.body: In which organisms is a PI4KIIalpha (PI4KIIα) orthologue present?
questions.ideal_answer: PI4KIIalpha (PI4KIIα) orthologues are present in a range of organisms. The enzyme is well characterized in mammalian cells broadly, with specific studies in humans, mice, and rats. A Drosophila PI4KII orthologue has been expressed and characterized, with depletion causing severe reduction of PtdIns 4-kinase activity. PI4K2A has also been studied in zebrafish larvae in the context of neurodevelopmental gene expression. Additional mammalian species include bovine mammary epithelial cells (dairy cows) and Pekin ducks, where PI4K2A was identified through lipidomic and transcriptomic analyses. Overall, PI4KIIα is considered a broadly conserved enzyme found across eukaryotic cells and organisms, underscoring its fundamental role in phosphatidylinositol 4-phosphate synthesis and intracellular trafficking.

questions.body: How often is hypertension present in patients with Cushing's Syndrome?
Please list the FDA approved Janus kinase inhibitors
questions.ideal_answer: Based on the provided abstracts, the FDA-approved Janus kinase (JAK) inhibitors include baricitinib and ritlecitinib, approved for severe alopecia areata. Momelotinib and pacritinib are approved for myelofibrosis. Ruxolitinib cream is approved for mild to moderate atopic dermatitis and nonsegmental vitiligo. Tofacitinib is an oral JAK inhibitor approved for rheumatoid arthritis and subject to FDA class warnings. Upadacitinib is approved for moderately to severely active Crohn's disease. Delgocitinib 2% cream is the first FDA-approved topical JAK inhibitor for moderate-to-severe chronic hand eczema. These approvals span both oral and topical formulations across a range of inflammatory and autoimmune conditions.

questions.body: How does protein localization to lipid droplets occur?
How do glucocorticoid withdrawal syndrome's symptoms evolve over time after surgical remission of Cushing's Syndrome?
Relative to intestinal disease, what are HETE and HODE
Can proteins acquire new functions after forming amyloid structures?
questions.ideal_answer: Yes, proteins can acquire new functions after forming amyloid structures. Multiple lines of evidence support this conclusion. Bacterial functional amyloids such as curli and FapC not only scaffold biofilms but also display intrinsic catalytic activity, expanding the biochemical repertoire of extracellular matrices beyond what the soluble proteins can do. Amelogenin forms amyloid nanoribbons that serve as templates for apatite growth during enamel biomineralization, a function distinct from the monomeric protein. Marine adhesive proteins form amyloid-like structures that confer strong adhesive properties suited for attachment in aquatic environments. The functional amyloid CRES in the epididymal matrix assembles into structurally heterogeneous forms that support host defense and sperm maturation, with DNA binding modulating assembly pathways to achieve functional diversity. Additionally, stress-inducible amyloid bodies (A-bodies) in eukaryotic cells induce a protective state of cellular dormancy, representing a gain-of-function response to environmental stress. Bacterial functional amyloids also facilitate adhesion, storage, dissemination, and virulence. Collectively, these examples demonstrate that amyloid formation is not merely an aggregation event but can confer entirely new biochemical, structural, and regulatory functions upon proteins.

questions.body: How often do patients fail to self-administer hydrocortisone injections in cases of acute adrenal crisis?
questions.ideal_answer: According to a 2022 survey of individuals with adrenal insufficiency conducted through the National Adrenal Diseases Foundation, nearly half (41%) of patients were unable to self-administer an emergency glucocorticoid injection during an acute adrenal crisis. The primary barriers cited were the effects of the crisis-associated illness itself and confusion experienced by the patients. Failed injections led to worse outcomes, including becoming sicker, requiring hospitalization, or death, in 36% of cases. These findings highlight that reliance solely on patient self-injection may result in worse outcomes, and that treating physicians should provide thorough patient education about injection technique, as well as preparation for the potential need for assistance during a crisis.

questions.body: What is a trichoscope used for?
questions.ideal_answer: A trichoscope is a noninvasive diagnostic tool used for the diagnosis and follow-up of hair and scalp disorders. It allows recognition of morphologic structures not visible to the naked eye, enabling detailed examination of hair and scalp. It is employed to evaluate causes of alopecia such as alopecia areata, tinea capitis, trichotillomania, and androgenetic alopecia, revealing distinct features like exclamation mark hairs, comma hairs, corkscrew hairs, yellow dots, and white dots. Trichoscopy facilitates early diagnosis of conditions like androgenetic alopecia and can assess disease severity. It also serves as a rapid noninvasive tool for diagnosing patchy hair loss in pediatric patients. Furthermore, trichoscopy helps decode underlying conditions and identify prognostic markers, such as the 'i hair' sign in alopecia areata and trichotillomania.
exact_answer: diagnosis and follow-up of hair and scalp disorders
evaluate the causes of focal non-cicatricial alopecia

questions.body: How many kinases belong to the serine/arginine kinase family of the human kinome?
questions.ideal_answer: The serine/arginine protein kinase (SRPK) family of the human kinome consists of three members: SRPK1, SRPK2, and SRPK3. SRPK1 and SRPK2 are well-established kinases that phosphorylate serine-arginine rich domains of SR proteins, regulating pre-mRNA splicing, and have been implicated in numerous cancers as well as viral infections such as hepatitis B. SRPK3 is a less-studied member of the family, but has been implicated in breast cancer; the development of selective chemical probes for SRPK3 has been reported, with compounds showing selectivity over SRPK1 and SRPK2. All three kinases share the ability to phosphorylate SR proteins and modulate alternative splicing, and their dysregulation is associated with oncogenesis and other disease states.

questions.body: What are the subtypes of Cushing Syndrome?
questions.ideal_answer: Cushing syndrome subtypes are broadly categorized as ACTH-dependent and ACTH-independent forms. ACTH-dependent Cushing syndrome includes Cushing's disease, caused by corticotroph pituitary tumors overproducing ACTH, and ectopic Cushing syndrome, resulting from ACTH production by extra-pituitary neuroendocrine tumors. ACTH-independent Cushing syndrome arises from autonomous cortisol production by adrenal lesions, including adrenal adenomas, hyperplasia, or carcinomas, collectively referred to as adrenal Cushing syndrome. Additional recognized forms include functional non-neoplastic hypercortisolism, formerly known as pseudo-Cushing state, mild autonomous cortisol secretion often identified in the context of adrenal incidentalomas, and bilateral adrenal adenomas. Recent advances have broadened the differential diagnosis spectrum, particularly with a new genetic classification of adrenal Cushing syndrome, underscoring the importance of precise subtyping to guide appropriate management strategies for each patient.

questions.body: What are the symptoms of TAFRO syndrome?
questions.ideal_answer: TAFRO syndrome is a rare systemic inflammatory disorder whose name is an acronym derived from its key symptoms. These include thrombocytopenia (low platelet count), anasarca (generalized edema or fluid retention throughout the body), fever, reticulin fibrosis (fibrosis of the bone marrow), renal dysfunction (kidney problems), and organomegaly (enlargement of organs such as the liver and spleen). The syndrome is associated with hypercytokinaemia and cytokine storms, and it overlaps clinically with idiopathic multicentric Castleman disease (iMCD). It can occur following infections such as COVID-19 and Epstein-Barr virus.

questions.body: What are the most common diseases caused by dysfunction of the ubiquitin system?
questions.ideal_answer: Dysfunction of the ubiquitin system is implicated in a range of serious human diseases, particularly neurodegenerative disorders and cancer. OTU deubiquitinase dysregulation is explicitly linked to cancer. Alzheimer's disease involves defects in USP10 function affecting tau pathology, as well as disruption of MLKL-USP7-UBA52 signaling that maintains ubiquitin homeostasis and autophagy. Parkinson's disease progression is associated with ubiquitin-mediated proteolysis pathways, with key biomarker genes such as LRRK2 playing roles in ubiquitin regulation. Huntington's disease is connected to impaired SUMOylation-mediated ubiquitination of mutant huntingtin protein and USP11 dysregulation affecting mitochondrial function and neuronal survival. Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis is linked to UBQLN2 ubiquitylation defects affecting proteasome-mediated degradation and to increased UFMylation, particularly of myosin, in skeletal muscle. Together, these examples illustrate how perturbations in ubiquitin system components, including E3 ligases, deubiquitinases, and ubiquitin-like modifiers, contribute broadly to neurodegeneration and oncogenesis.

questions.body: What is the cortisol awakening response (CAR)?
What  type of  blood cell has  CD19 as a surface biomarker?
questions.ideal_answer: CD19 is a surface biomarker specifically expressed on B cells. It is a transmembrane glycoprotein, also known as the B-lymphocyte antigen, that serves as a B cell-specific surface marker. CD19 is expressed on the majority of neoplastic B cells and has emerged as an attractive biomarker and therapeutic target for B-cell malignancies. Studies investigating peroxisome biogenesis in human peripheral blood mononuclear cells specifically analyzed CD19+ B cells, distinguishing them from CD4+ T cells, CD8+ T cells, and CD14+ monocytes, further confirming CD19 as a B cell-specific marker.

questions.body: What are the most common vectors used in gene therapy?
questions.ideal_answer: The most common vectors used in gene therapy fall into two broad categories: viral and non-viral. Among viral vectors, adeno-associated viruses (AAV) are currently the leading platform, accounting for five of seven FDA-approved gene therapies since 2021, and are used across hematologic, neuromuscular, dermatologic, and neurogenetic diseases. Lentiviruses are widely used for their ability to carry larger gene sequences and provide semipermanent expression, particularly in neural and oncologic applications. Adenoviruses and retroviruses are also prevalent in clinical settings, while herpes simplex virus vectors are explored in cancer trials. Baculoviral vectors have shown promise in cardiovascular applications such as myocardial infarction. Non-viral delivery systems are an increasingly important alternative, including liposomes and liposomal-derived technologies such as solid lipid nanoparticles, polymer nanoparticles, dendrimers, and polymeric micelles, which offer high loading capacity and the ability to deliver nucleic acids, mRNA, or proteins directly. Minicircle DNA represents another non-viral approach with a favorable safety profile. While viral vectors currently dominate clinical gene therapy, non-viral systems continue to advance as complementary or alternative delivery platforms.

questions.body: What is the time to diagnosis for adrenal Cushing's Syndrome?
questions.ideal_answer: According to a meta-analysis based on 5367 patients assessing time to diagnosis in Cushing's Syndrome, the mean time to diagnosis for adrenal Cushing's Syndrome was 30 months. This differs from ectopic CS, which had the shortest mean time to diagnosis at 14 months, and pituitary CS, which had the longest at 38 months. The overall mean time to diagnosis across all subtypes of Cushing's Syndrome was 34 months. The study found no significant differences based on gender, age, or year of diagnosis, but noted that time to diagnosis remains long and requires improvement to prevent complications associated with delayed treatment.

questions.body: What is the indication for Brineura?
questions.ideal_answer: Brineura (cerliponase alfa) is indicated to slow the loss of ambulation in pediatric patients with neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis type 2 (CLN2), a pediatric neurodegenerative lysosomal storage disorder caused by a deficiency in tripeptidyl peptidase-1 (TPP1). It was first approved in the USA for the treatment of motor function loss in pediatric patients aged 3 years and older with CLN2, and subsequently received approval in the EU for CLN2 in patients of all ages. CLN2 is characterized by progressive impairment of motor function, language deficiencies, seizures, ataxia, blindness, and early death, and intracerebroventricular infusion of cerliponase alfa has been shown to reduce the progression of functional decline.
exact_answer: to slow the loss of ambulation in pediatric patients with CLN2
treatment of motor function loss in paediatric patients ≥3 years of age with CLN2

questions.body: What is the primary energy source for cancer cells?
questions.ideal_answer: Glucose is the primary energy source for cancer cells. Multiple studies confirm that cancer cells exhibit metabolic reprogramming characterized by the Warburg effect, which describes the preferential use of aerobic glycolysis over oxidative phosphorylation even under normoxic conditions. This heavy reliance on glucose supports rapid proliferation, survival, and migration of cancer cells. Glucose is transported into cancer cells via transporters such as GLUT1 and GLUT5, and its metabolism produces lactate as a major byproduct, which itself can serve as an alternative energy source via monocarboxylate transporters. Glutamine is recognized as the second most utilized energy source after glucose, supporting cancer cell proliferation through glutaminolysis. Cancer cells also demonstrate metabolic plasticity, enabling them to utilize other substrates including lipids and amino acids when glucose is limited. Nevertheless, glucose remains the principal and most frequently cited fuel source across studies of cancer metabolism, and therapeutic strategies targeting glucose uptake and glycolysis, such as glucose oxidase-based nanocatalytic platforms and GLUT inhibitors, underscore its central importance in sustaining tumor growth.

questions.body: Are adrenocortical secretory tumors in humans mostly a hereditary disorder?
questions.ideal_answer: Adrenocortical secretory tumors in humans are not mostly a hereditary disorder. According to the available evidence, such tumors are mostly sporadic in nature, meaning they arise randomly rather than through inherited genetic predisposition. While they can occasionally be associated with hereditary syndromes that predispose individuals to tumor development, this represents the minority of cases. When these tumors do secrete hormones, they can cause clinical presentations such as Cushing syndrome, primary aldosteronism, virilization, or feminization. The majority are adenomas, though some can be malignant carcinomas with poor prognosis. Management typically requires surgery, with laparoscopic approaches now widely used for benign tumors.

questions.body: Please describe alopecia areata.
What is the intracellular localization of PI4KIIalpha in human cells?
questions.ideal_answer: PI4KIIalpha localizes to multiple intracellular compartments in human cells. It is most prominently associated with the trans-Golgi network and broader Golgi complex, including a juxtanuclear membrane pool and perinuclear region. PI4KIIalpha is also found on exocytotic vesicles and synaptic vesicles, and is concentrated at synapses and in neuronal perikarya. It resides in LAMP-1-positive endosomes and AP-3-positive organelles, including both perinuclear and peripheral organelles and peripheral membranes, as well as nerve terminals. The enzyme associates with lipid rafts, and its membrane targeting to the TGN and lipid rafts is dependent on palmitoylation of its CCPCC motif. Additionally, PI4KIIalpha can be detected at the cellular surface and within other intracellular compartments, with its distribution influenced by cholesterol levels, AP-3 interactions, and palmitoylation state.

questions.body: What is the prevalence of secondary adrenal insufficiency?
questions.ideal_answer: The prevalence of secondary adrenal insufficiency is estimated to be between 150-280 per million people. The most frequent cause is believed to be corticosteroid-induced insufficiency, although the clinical relevance of deficiency after cessation of glucocorticoid treatment is widely debated. For comparison, primary adrenal insufficiency has a lower estimated prevalence of 82-144 per million, with autoimmunity being the most common cause in adults and genetic causes being most common in children.

questions.body: SynNeurGe classification system is associated with what disease?
questions.ideal_answer: The SynNeurGe classification system is associated with Parkinson's disease. It is a biologically based classification proposed to address the complexity and heterogeneity of Parkinson's disease by integrating three components: presence or absence of pathological α-synuclein (S) in tissues or CSF, evidence of underlying neurodegeneration (N) defined by neuroimaging procedures, and documentation of pathogenic gene variants (G) that cause or strongly predispose to Parkinson's disease. These three components are linked to a clinical component (C). The system aims to facilitate both basic and clinical research and move the field toward precision medicine required to develop disease-modifying therapies. Its initial application is recommended exclusively for research settings, with prospective validation deemed necessary to ensure efficacy and ethical application in clinical practice.

questions.body: How is the pathogenic prion protein formed from the normal prion protein?
What is the average delay in the diagnosis of Cushing's Syndrome?
questions.ideal_answer: The average delay in the diagnosis of Cushing's Syndrome is approximately 34 months, based on a meta-analysis of 5367 patients. This delay varies by subtype: ectopic CS has the shortest time to diagnosis at 14 months, adrenal CS at 30 months, and pituitary CS at 38 months. No significant difference was found based on gender, age, or year of diagnosis. Other studies report a median time to diagnosis of 24 months in patients undergoing adrenalectomy, and data from the European Registry on Cushing's Syndrome (ERCUSYN) noted a delay of approximately 2 years between symptom onset and diagnosis. A patient survey found that 49% of patients experienced a diagnostic delay exceeding 2 years. This prolonged delay is largely attributed to the overlap of Cushing's Syndrome symptoms with common conditions such as metabolic syndrome, obesity, osteoporosis, and depression, leading to multiple specialist consultations before a correct diagnosis is reached.

questions.body: Which of the 5 senses does otospongiosis affect?
questions.ideal_answer: Otospongiosis, also known as otosclerosis, affects the sense of hearing. It is a primary osteodystrophy of the otic capsule of the inner ear and one of the leading causes of deafness in adults. It commonly causes conductive hearing loss due to fixation of the stapes footplate, and may also present with sensorineural or mixed hearing loss when the cochlea is involved. Medical treatments such as sodium fluoride and bisphosphonates aim to stabilize hearing thresholds and slow disease progression, while surgical intervention such as stapedotomy is the definitive treatment to improve hearing outcomes by closing the air-bone gap.

questions.body: How is terminal differentiation regulated?
What are the common symptoms of adrenal insufficiency?
questions.ideal_answer: Common symptoms of adrenal insufficiency include general weakness, fatigue, anorexia, arthralgia, fever, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, myalgias, weight loss, and salt craving. Hyponatremia and hyperkalemia are common laboratory findings. Without treatment, patients may develop hyperpigmented skin and mucus membranes. During an acute adrenal crisis, which is a medical emergency, symptoms can include hypotension, lethargy, tachycardia, nausea, vomiting, and hypoglycemia. These symptoms develop slowly over several months in chronic adrenal insufficiency, related to alterations in salt and water balance, while acute adrenal crisis requires prompt treatment with intravenous fluids, blood pressure support, and stress dosing of intravenous hydrocortisone.

questions.body: Krabbe disease, also known as globoid-cell leukodystrophy is thought to results from what toxic metabolite?
questions.ideal_answer: Krabbe disease, also known as globoid-cell leukodystrophy, is caused by a functional deficiency of the lysosomal enzyme galactocerebrosidase (GALC), also referred to as galactosylceramidase. This deficiency leads to the progressive accumulation of the toxic metabolite psychosine. Psychosine accumulation affects many different cellular pathways, leading to severe demyelination of both the central and peripheral nervous systems. Additionally, psychosine has been shown to potentiate inflammatory responses, including increased TNF-alpha production by peripheral blood mononuclear cells in Krabbe disease patients, further contributing to the pathogenesis of the disease.

Is Calcium dyshomeostasis  assoiciated with Huntington's disease?
questions.ideal_answer: Yes, calcium dyshomeostasis is associated with Huntington's disease (HD). Multiple lines of evidence support this connection. HD is caused by expanded CAG repeats in the huntingtin gene, and the resulting mutant huntingtin protein disrupts neuronal calcium homeostasis, which is considered an important factor linking the mutation to disease onset and progression. Mitochondrial calcium dyshomeostasis is specifically implicated in HD pathogenesis, alongside impaired mitochondrial energetics and oxidative stress. Furthermore, HD is characterized by a positive feedback loop between calcium dyshomeostasis and huntingtin protein aggregation, which escalates over time and ultimately leads to neuronal loss, particularly in the striatum. The calcium dyshomeostasis in HD encompasses mitochondrial and endoplasmic reticulum dysfunction, impaired calcium buffering, glutamate excitotoxicity, and alterations in calcium entry routes into neurons. These findings highlight calcium dyshomeostasis as a central mechanism in HD pathogenesis and a potential therapeutic target.

questions.body: What are the most common mechanisms leading to amyloid formation?
questions.ideal_answer: Amyloid formation arises through several interconnected mechanisms. At the molecular level, charge-charge interactions and salting-out effects stabilize compact conformers and accelerate aggregation, while disruption of the supersaturated state above thermodynamic solubility can trigger fibril nucleation. Conformational transitions, such as shifts from unfolded monomeric states to molten globule-like intermediates, also promote amyloid assembly. At the cellular and systemic level, an imbalance between in vivo amyloid production and natural clearance drives disease progression. Protein misfolding and aggregation are fundamental, often initiated by genetic mutations or single nucleotide polymorphisms, as seen with LECT2 and APP. Membrane protein dimerization, particularly of the amyloid precursor protein, regulates amyloid peptide production and subsequent aggregation into oligomers and fibrils. Additionally, metabolic stress can promote amyloid formation, for example by increasing IAPP aggregation in pancreatic beta cells. Together, these mechanisms collectively determine the amyloidogenic landscape across diverse diseases including Alzheimer's disease, type 2 diabetes, and transthyretin amyloid cardiomyopathy.
exact_answer: disrupting the supersaturated state above the thermodynamic solubility
conformational transitions from unfolded to molten globule-like states
imbalance between in vivo amyloid production and natural clearance

is Paracoccidioidomycosis caused by a parasite?
questions.ideal_answer: Paracoccidioidomycosis is not caused by a parasite. It is a systemic mycosis, meaning it is a fungal disease, caused by the dimorphic fungus Paracoccidioides brasiliensis and related species (Paracoccidioides spp.). It is endemic to Latin America, particularly affecting rural and socioeconomically vulnerable communities. The disease is classified as a neglected tropical fungal disease, and all three abstracts consistently describe it as a mycosis rather than a parasitic infection. The causative agent grows as a yeast form in host tissues and can be cultured on routine media, as demonstrated in one case where Paracoccidioides brasiliensis was isolated from chronic finger lesions. Fungi and parasites are distinct categories of organisms, and Paracoccidioides belongs firmly to the fungal kingdom, making the answer to whether paracoccidioidomycosis is caused by a parasite clearly no.

questions.body: What are the most common bacteria in the human skin microbiome?
questions.ideal_answer: The human skin microbiome is dominated by several bacterial genera and species. Staphylococcus is among the most prevalent, with Staphylococcus epidermidis being a ubiquitous commensal; other common species include S. capitis, S. hominis, and S. warneri. Streptococcus species, particularly S. mitis and S. parasanguinis, are also frequently identified in high abundance. Corynebacterium is another major component, with Corynebacterium tuberculostearicum and C. bovis being commonly found, alongside newly described species such as C. axilliensis and C. jamesii. Cutibacterium, especially Cutibacterium acnes, is a predominant member of the facial skin microbiome. Micrococcus, including Micrococcus luteus, and Kocuria are consistently identified as core skin microbiome genera. Neisseriaceae has also been noted as a predominant family in certain microbiome profiles, and Bacillus species have been isolated from skin as well. Together, these taxa represent the core bacterial community of the human skin microbiome across different body sites.

questions.body: What is the mean time to diagnosis for ectopic Cushing's Syndrome?
questions.ideal_answer: According to a meta-analysis based on 5367 patients, the mean time to diagnosis for ectopic Cushing's Syndrome was 14 months. This was the shortest among the subtypes of Cushing's Syndrome, compared to adrenal Cushing's Syndrome at 30 months and pituitary Cushing's Syndrome at 38 months. The overall mean time to diagnosis across all subtypes was 34 months. No significant differences in time to diagnosis were found based on gender, age, or year of diagnosis (before or after 2000), though patients with pituitary Cushing's Syndrome in Germany had a longer time to diagnosis than elsewhere.

Is the inactive X chromosome (Barr body) less susceptible to genotoxic damage or mutations than the active X chromosome?
questions.ideal_answer: The inactive X chromosome (Xi) is not less susceptible to genotoxic damage or mutations compared to the active X chromosome. Studies show that both active and inactive X chromosomes exhibit similar radiosensitivity, meaning a comparable number of DNA breaks are induced in each following irradiation (Abstract 8). While repair hierarchies exist where inactive genes are repaired more slowly than active genes after UV damage (Abstracts 6 and 7), this reflects differences in repair efficiency rather than reduced initial susceptibility to damage. In fact, slower repair of the inactive X may lead to more persistent damage and potentially more mutations, not fewer. The inactive X also undergoes a conserved DNA damage response, including chromatin relaxation and repair factor recruitment, indicating it actively responds to damage (Abstract 3). Furthermore, exogenous DNA damage can disrupt epigenetic marks on the Xi, such as reduced H3K27me3 deposition, further destabilizing its silenced state (Abstract 4). Collectively, the evidence indicates that the Xi is neither protected from initial genotoxic insult nor more efficiently repaired, and thus cannot be considered less susceptible to genotoxic damage or mutations than the active X chromosome.

Please list the common side effects of Kymriah
questions.ideal_answer: Based on the provided context, the common side effects associated with Kymriah (tisagenlecleucel), an anti-CD19 CAR T-cell therapy, include cytokine release syndrome (CRS) and CAR T-cell-related encephalopathy syndrome (CRES). These toxicities can be severe or even fatal, and their careful management is considered vital for patients undergoing this treatment for relapsed or refractory B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia.

questions.body: Which factors trigger eustress responses in the body?
questions.ideal_answer: Eustress responses in the body are triggered by a variety of factors. Regular physical activity generates reactive oxygen species (ROS) through mitochondrial and NADPH oxidase systems, initiating adaptive redox signaling pathways. Environmental enrichment (EE), which provides enhanced sensory, cognitive, motor, and social stimulation, induces eustress via hormetic mechanisms that promote neuroplasticity and stress resilience. In humans, analogous triggers include cognitive challenges, social engagement, and music. Small doses of stressful stimuli more broadly can mobilize antioxidant defense mechanisms through hormetic or preconditioning responses. At the molecular level, compounds such as luteolin activate Nrf2 signaling, contributing to cellular redox balance. Environmental enrichment also triggers upregulation of beneficial gut microbiota, particularly Lactobacillus spp., which mediates systemic anti-tumor and immune responses. Together, these factors illustrate that eustress is induced by mild, controllable stimuli ranging from exercise and environmental enrichment to specific phytochemicals and microbial modulation, all promoting adaptive responses without causing harmful distress.
exact_answer: upregulation of Lactobacillus spp. in gut microbiota

questions.body: Is Cushing Syndrome present in canine populations?
questions.ideal_answer: Yes, Cushing's syndrome (naturally occurring hypercortisolism) is well-documented in canine populations. It is described as a common endocrine disorder in dogs, with an estimated 1-year period prevalence of 0.17% and an annual incidence of 0.06% among dogs under UK primary veterinary care. The condition arises primarily from ACTH-producing pituitary adenomas (pituitary-dependent hypercortisolism, accounting for 80-85% of cases) or adrenocortical tumors (15-20% of cases). Certain breeds carry higher risk, including Bichon frises, Border terriers, and Miniature schnauzers, while Golden retrievers and Labrador retrievers appear more protected. Additional risk factors include increasing age and body weight above the breed-sex mean. Diagnosis can be challenging due to variable clinical presentation, and machine learning tools have been developed to assist with screening. Treatment options include surgical removal of the causative tumor, radiotherapy, and pharmacotherapy with drugs such as trilostane or mitotane, with ongoing research into more targeted therapies.

questions.body: Does a zoonosis affect humans?
questions.ideal_answer: Yes, zoonoses affect humans. By definition, a zoonosis is a disease that can be transmitted from animals to humans, and the provided abstracts offer multiple clear examples. Western equine encephalitis is described as a zoonotic disease in which humans are accidental hosts, capable of causing meningoencephalitis, particularly in children. Spotted fever group rickettsioses, including Rickettsia heilongjiangensis, are identified as a significant zoonotic threat to public health. Tularemia, caused by Francisella tularensis, is a nationally notifiable zoonosis in the United States, with 2,462 cases reported between 2011 and 2022, causing fever and localized symptoms with a case fatality rate that can exceed 2% depending on the strain and clinical manifestation. Crimean-Congo hemorrhagic fever is a tick-borne zoonotic disease present in many African countries, with serological evidence of human exposure in Tanzania linked to livestock activities such as milking. Taken together, these examples confirm that zoonotic diseases can and do affect humans, sometimes causing severe or fatal illness.

questions.body: How do larval salivary glands differ from adult salivary glands in Drosophila melanogaster?
How often is leukocytosis present in patients with Cushing's Syndrome?
questions.ideal_answer: Leukocytosis is frequently observed in patients with Cushing's Syndrome, though reported prevalence varies by study. A smaller study of 26 patients with Cushing's disease found elevated white blood cell counts in approximately 40% of cases (11/28 events), with mean baseline WBC of 10,500 cells/µl dropping significantly after treatment. A larger nationwide retrospective cohort study of 297 CS patients reported leukocytosis in 21.5% of patients at baseline, compared to 8.9% in matched controls. Both studies confirmed significantly higher WBC counts, neutrophil counts, and neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratios in CS patients compared to controls, regardless of pituitary or adrenal source of hypercortisolemia. After surgical treatment, WBC counts decreased significantly, though they did not fully normalize in all patients even after remission. Notably, baseline leukocytosis was associated with a lower remission rate in Cushing's disease patients (36.7% vs. 63.9%), suggesting it may serve as a prognostic marker.

questions.body: What are the hallmarks of Acro-cardio-facial syndrome (ACFS)
questions.ideal_answer: Acro-cardio-facial syndrome (ACFS) is a rare genetic disorder with a broad constellation of congenital anomalies. The primary hallmarks include split-hand/split-foot malformation (ectrodactyly), congenital heart defects, cleft lip and palate, and facial anomalies. Genital anomalies, mental retardation or intellectual disability, and growth retardation are also consistent features of the syndrome. Additional findings that may expand the phenotypic spectrum include mild scoliosis and hemivertebrae. An autosomal recessive inheritance pattern has been suggested based on parental consanguinity and recurrence in siblings, and some evidence points to a microdeletion at chromosome 6q21-q22.3 as a possible underlying cause in certain cases. Prognosis is generally poor, with most reported patients not surviving the first days or months of life.

What is the mean time to diagnosis for pituitary Chushing's Syndrome?
questions.ideal_answer: According to a meta-analysis based on 5367 patients, the mean time to diagnosis for pituitary Cushing's Syndrome was 38 months. This is the longest among the subtypes of Cushing's Syndrome, compared to adrenal CS (30 months) and ectopic CS (14 months). The overall mean time to diagnosis across all subtypes was 34 months. No significant differences were found based on gender, age, or year of diagnosis (before or after 2000), though patients with pituitary CS in Germany had a longer time to diagnosis than elsewhere.

questions.body: Has Vitamin D therapy been shown to improve cognitive function in Alzheimers disease?
questions.ideal_answer: While observational studies suggest an association between higher vitamin D levels and better cognitive performance, and lower vitamin D levels have been consistently linked to Alzheimer's disease, rigorous Mendelian randomization analyses found no clear causal evidence that increasing vitamin D concentrations improves cognitive function. The MR study, which mitigates confounding bias inherent in observational research, showed null effects of genetically estimated vitamin D levels on cognitive measures including PACC scores, both in the general population and in individuals over 60. Cross-sectional data confirm that AD and vascular dementia patients have significantly lower vitamin D levels than cognitively normal individuals, but this association does not establish that vitamin D therapy reverses or improves cognition. Some mechanistic research highlights potential neuroprotective roles of active vitamin D through anti-inflammatory pathways in brain pericytes and various cellular signaling mechanisms, suggesting biological plausibility, but these findings do not constitute clinical evidence of cognitive improvement. In summary, current evidence does not support that vitamin D therapy has been shown to improve cognitive function in Alzheimer's disease, and the most methodologically robust studies find no causal benefit.

questions.body: Can AlphaFold be used in a reverse manner to predict a possible amino acid sequence from a desired protein structure (inverse protein folding)?
questions.ideal_answer: Yes, AlphaFold can be used in approaches to solve the inverse protein folding problem, which involves predicting an amino acid sequence from a desired protein structure. While AlphaFold itself is a forward-folding model that predicts structure from sequence, it plays a central role in inverse folding workflows. As described in Abstract 4, AlphaFold's confidence metrics such as pTM and pLDDT can be distilled into faster, end-to-end differentiable models that serve as structure consistency regularizers during training of inverse folding models, yielding improvements in sequence recovery and protein diversity. Abstract 7 describes SeqPredNN, a neural network that predicts amino acid sequences expected to fold into specified structures, using AlphaFold to validate the predicted folds by confirming structural similarity to the target. Additionally, Abstracts 5 and 6 describe complementary inverse folding methods that leverage structural information to design sequences. Together, these approaches demonstrate that AlphaFold, whether directly or through distillation of its outputs, is integral to the emerging field of inverse protein folding and computational protein design.

questions.body: Is Cushing syndrome present in feline populations?
questions.ideal_answer: Yes, Cushing syndrome (hyperadrenocorticism) is present in feline populations. While it is diagnosed far less frequently in cats than in dogs, it is a well-recognized adrenal disorder in cats, with an incidence probably similar to that in humans. Clinical signs unique to cats include unregulated diabetes mellitus and severe skin atrophy, while other manifestations such as a potbellied appearance, polydipsia, polyuria, and increased susceptibility to infections are shared with dogs. Clinicians must be aware that diagnostic tests and treatments for feline hyperadrenocorticism differ from those used in dogs, making familiarity with species-specific approaches essential for proper management.

questions.body: Is Heck's disease caused by a bacterium?
questions.ideal_answer: Heck's disease is not caused by a bacterium. Also known as focal epithelial hyperplasia (FEH), it is caused by the human papillomavirus (HPV), which induces proliferation of epithelial cells in the oral mucosa. The condition typically presents as asymptomatic, soft, elevated, sessile, and lobulated lesions and is more prevalent among children and within families. Diagnosis is confirmed through clinical and histopathological examination, and treatment options include surgical removal, such as with a diode laser, which has shown effective results with no scarring and no recurrence during follow-up.

questions.body: Is situs inversus totalis compatible with life?
questions.ideal_answer: Yes, situs inversus totalis (SIT) is compatible with life. Multiple sources confirm that SIT generally does not have pathophysiological significance, and patients with SIT do not have a decreased survival rate compared to healthy individuals. It is a rare congenital anomaly with an incidence of approximately 1 in 8,000 to 1 in 25,000 live births, involving complete right-to-left reversal of thoracic and abdominal organs. The condition is often diagnosed incidentally when patients present for other medical concerns, and patients can live normal lives. For example, a 93-year-old woman with SIT underwent emergency surgery successfully, and a 34-year-old diabetic woman with SIT was counseled that she could live a normal life. While SIT can be associated with conditions such as primary ciliary dyskinesia, which may complicate health outcomes, SIT itself is not life-threatening. Clinicians must be aware of the anatomical variation to avoid diagnostic pitfalls and to plan surgical interventions carefully, but the condition itself does not reduce life expectancy.

questions.body: What is the treatment of choice for Cushing's Disease?
questions.ideal_answer: The treatment of choice for Cushing's disease (CD) is trans-sphenoidal surgery (TSS). Although TSS is the preferred initial treatment, it is not always curative and is associated with a substantial rate of recurrence, with published recurrence rates varying between 5 and 20%. Approximately half of these recurrences appear within 5 years post-surgery, and the remaining half within or even after 10 years. A low or undetectable cortisol level in the immediate post-operative period is regarded as the best criterion of remission, though it does not accurately predict long-term outcomes. Current guidelines recommend that every patient in remission be monitored for possible recurrence using methods such as late night salivary cortisol, dexamethasone suppression testing, and urinary free cortisol measurement.

What is the difference between Cushing Syndrome and Cushing Disease?
questions.ideal_answer: Cushing's syndrome refers to the broader condition of sustained pathologic hypercortisolism, which can arise from multiple causes: excessive corticotropin (ACTH) secretion by tumors in the pituitary gland (Cushing's disease, approximately 70% of cases), ectopic ACTH secretion from tumors elsewhere (about 15%), or ACTH-independent cortisol secretion directly from adrenal tumors (about 15%). Cushing's disease is therefore a specific subtype of Cushing's syndrome in which the hypercortisolism is caused exclusively by a pituitary tumor secreting excess ACTH. Differentiating a pituitary source of excess ACTH (Cushing's disease) from an ectopic source is accomplished by imaging the pituitary and sampling for ACTH in the venous drainage of the pituitary. The prognosis is better for Cushing's disease and benign adrenal causes of Cushing's syndrome than for adrenocortical cancer and malignant ACTH-producing tumors.
exact_answer: Cushing's disease is a specific subtype of Cushing's syndrome caused by excess ACTH secretion from a pituitary tumor, while Cushing's syndrome is the broader condition of sustained pathologic hypercortisolism from any cause
Cushing's disease accounts for approximately 70% of Cushing's syndrome cases
Cushing's syndrome can also result from ectopic ACTH secretion or ACTH-independent cortisol secretion from adrenal tumors
